_comment: Major annual meteor showers visible from the Northern Hemisphere. Update yearly with current peak dates from the IMO calendar (https://www.imo.net/resources/calendar/). 'peak' is in YYYY-MM-DD format for the current year. 'window_start' and 'window_end' bracket the active period when meteors are still visible above background rates.

showers.name: Quadrantids
showers.notes: One of the strongest annual showers but the peak is short — only 6 hours of high activity. Cold January nights make this one for the dedicated.

showers.name: Lyrids
showers.best_viewing: Late evening through pre-dawn, look east-northeast
showers.notes: Modest but reliable. Known for occasional bright fireballs. First good shower of spring.

showers.name: Eta Aquariids
showers.notes: Debris from Halley's Comet. Better from southern latitudes but still rewarding from the southern US. Look in the hour before sunrise.

showers.name: Southern Delta Aquariids
showers.notes: Warm-weather shower with a long, broad peak. Good warm-up act for the Perseids.

showers.name: Perseids
showers.best_viewing: After midnight through pre-dawn, look northeast
showers.notes: The big one. Warm August nights, reliable 60-100 meteors per hour at peak, often with bright fireballs. The shower most people have actually seen.

showers.name: Draconids
showers.best_viewing: Early evening, look high in the north
showers.notes: Unusual evening shower (most are pre-dawn). Modest most years but capable of brief outbursts of hundreds per hour.

showers.name: Orionids
showers.best_viewing: After midnight through pre-dawn, look east
showers.notes: Also from Halley's Comet. Fast meteors, often leaving trails. Steady performer through late October.

showers.name: Leonids
showers.notes: Modest most years but historically capable of meteor storms (1833, 1966, 2001). Famous for fast, bright meteors.

showers.name: Geminids
showers.notes: The strongest annual shower. Active early enough that you don't have to stay up until dawn. Multi-colored meteors. Worth bundling up for.

showers.name: Ursids
showers.notes: Modest end-of-year shower. Often overlooked because it falls right after the Geminids.
